subject: Chemistry
q: An organic compound undergoes first order decomposition. The time taken for decomposition to $\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)^{\text {th }}$ and $\left(\frac{1}{10}\right)^{\text {th }}$ of its initial concentration are $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 8}$ and $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 10}$ respectively. What is the value of $\frac{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 8}}{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 10}} \times 10$ ? ($\log 2=0.3$)
question: An organic compound undergoes first order decomposition. The time taken for decomposition to $\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)^{\text {th }}$ and $\left(\frac{1}{10}\right)^{\text {th }}$ of its initial concentration are $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 8}$ and $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 10}$ respectively. What is the value of $\frac{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 8}}{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 10}} \times 10$ ? ($\log 2=0.3$)
solution:
For first-order decomposition with rate equation $[A] = [A]_0 e^{-kt}$, the time for concentration to reduce to fraction f is: $t = \frac{1}{k}\ln\frac{1}{f} = \frac{\ln(1/f)}{k}$
For reduction to 1/8: $t_{1/8} = \frac{\ln 8}{k} = \frac{3\ln 2}{k}$
For reduction to 1/10: $t_{1/10} = \frac{\ln 10}{k}$
Therefore: $\frac{t_{1/8}}{t_{1/10}} = \frac{\ln 8}{\ln 10} = \frac{3\log 2}{\log 10} = \frac{3 \times 0.3}{1} = 0.9$
Thus: $\frac{t_{1/8}}{t_{1/10}} \times 10 = 0.9 \times 10 = 9$

subject: Chemistry
q:
A first order reaction has the rate constant,  k=4.6×10-3 s-1. The number of correct statement/s from the following is/are Given: log 3=0.48.

A. Reaction completes in 1000 s.
B. The reaction has a half-life of 500 s.
C. The time required for 10% completion is 25 times the time required for 90% completion.
D. The degree of dissociation is equal to 1-e-kt.
E. The rate and the rate constant have the same unit.
question:
A first order reaction has the rate constant,  k=4.6×10-3 s-1. The number of correct statement/s from the following is/are Given: log 3=0.48.

A. Reaction completes in 1000 s.
B. The reaction has a half-life of 500 s.
C. The time required for 10% completion is 25 times the time required for 90% completion.
D. The degree of dissociation is equal to 1-e-kt.
E. The rate and the rate constant have the same unit.
solution:
For a first order reaction:
t12 = 0.693k = 0.6934.6×10-3 s-1 = 150.65 s

For a first order reaction,
t10%=1Klnaa-x=1Kln10090

t10%=2.303 K(log 10-log 9)

t10%=2.093 K×(0.04)


Similarly

t90%=1 Kln10010

t90%=2.303 K

t90%t10%=10.04=25


We know,
ekt=aa-x

a-xa=e-kt

x=a1-e-kt

α=xa=1-e-kt

Thus, only option (D) is correct.

subject: Chemistry
q: The rate of a reaction A doubles on increasing the temperature from<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>to</mi><mo> </mo><mn>310</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. By how much, the temperature of reaction B should be increased from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> so that rate doubles if activation energy of the reaction B is twice to that of reaction A.
question: The rate of a reaction A doubles on increasing the temperature from<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>to</mi><mo> </mo><mn>310</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. By how much, the temperature of reaction B should be increased from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> so that rate doubles if activation energy of the reaction B is twice to that of reaction A.
solution:
log 2=EaR1300-1310 .......(i)

log  2=2EaR 1300-1T ......(ii)

2EaR 1300-1T=EaR 1300-1310

1300+1310=2T

⇒     T=300×310610×2

=304.92

Hence, the temperature of reaction B should be increased from 300 K by 304.92−300=4.92 K.

subject: Chemistry
q: Given above is the concentration vs time plot for a dissociation reaction : $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{nB}$. Based on the data of the initial phase of the reaction (initial 10 min), the value of n is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Given above is the concentration vs time plot for a dissociation reaction : $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{nB}$. Based on the data of the initial phase of the reaction (initial 10 min), the value of n is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
$A \rightarrow nB$

At $t = 0$: $[A] = 0.05$ M, $[B] = 0$
At $t = 10$ min: $[A] = 0.04$ M, $[B] = 0.01 \times n$

From the graph, $[B]$ at $t = 10$ min $= 0.03$ M

$0.01 \times n = 0.03$
$n = 3$

subject: Chemistry
q:
For a reaction \(\mathrm{A} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{K}_1} \mathrm{~B} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{K}_2} \mathrm{C}\)
If the rate of formation of \(B\) is set to be zero then the concentration of \(B\) is given by :
question:
For a reaction \(\mathrm{A} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{K}_1} \mathrm{~B} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{K}_2} \mathrm{C}\)
If the rate of formation of \(B\) is set to be zero then the concentration of \(B\) is given by :
solution:
Rate of formation of $B$ is
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{B}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{k}_1[\mathrm{~A}]-\mathrm{k}_2[\mathrm{~B}] \\ & 0=\mathrm{k}_1[\mathrm{~A}]-\mathrm{k}_2[\mathrm{~B}] \\ & \left(\frac{\mathrm{k}_1}{\mathrm{k}_2}\right)[\mathrm{A}]=[\mathrm{B}]\end{aligned}$

question:
A flask is filled with equal moles of A and B. The half lives of A and B are 100 s and 50 s respectively and are independent of the initial concentration. The time required for the concentration of A to be four times that of B is____s.
(Given : ln2=0.693)
solution:
For first order reaction

k=0.693t12

A=4B

A0e-kAt=4B0e-kBt

∵ as A0=B0;e-kAt=4e-kBt

-kAt=ln4-kBt

∴ tkB-kA=2ln2

t=2×0.6930.69350-0.693100=2×1002-1=200sec

subject: Chemistry
q: The half-life of a radioactive isotope is three hours. If the initial mass of the isotope were $256 \mathrm{~g}$, the mass of it remaining undecayed after 18 hours would be
question: The half-life of a radioactive isotope is three hours. If the initial mass of the isotope were $256 \mathrm{~g}$, the mass of it remaining undecayed after 18 hours would be
solution:
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=3 \mathrm{hrs}$. Initial mass $\left(\mathrm{C}_0\right)=256 \mathrm{~g}$
$\therefore \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_0}{2^{\mathrm{n}}}=\frac{256}{(2)^6}=\frac{256}{64}=4 \mathrm{~g}$

solution:

⇒A010A20=0.040.03=43

From t=2.303klog A10A2010= 2.303klog 4310= 2.303k(0.6020-0.4771)k= 2.30310×0.1249=2.876×10-2 min-1

t1/2=0.693k=0.6932.876×10-2=0.24×102 min=24 min.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The first order rate constant for the decomposition of CaCO3 at 700 K is 6.36×10-3 s-1 and activation energy is 209 kJ mol-1. Its rate constant (in s-1 at 600 K is x×10-6. The value of x is (Nearest integer)
[Given R=8.31 J K-1 mol-1;log6.36×10-3=-2.19,10-4.79=1.62×10-5
question:
The first order rate constant for the decomposition of CaCO3 at 700 K is 6.36×10-3 s-1 and activation energy is 209 kJ mol-1. Its rate constant (in s-1 at 600 K is x×10-6. The value of x is (Nearest integer)
[Given R=8.31 J K-1 mol-1;log6.36×10-3=-2.19,10-4.79=1.62×10-5
solution:
K700=6.36×10-3 s-1

K600=x×10-6 s-1

Ea=209 kJ/mol

Applying :

logKT2 KT1=-Ea2.303R1 T2-1 T1

logK700 K600=-Ea2.303R1700-1600

log6.36×10-3 K600=+209×10002.303×8.31100700×600

log6.36×10-3-logK600=2.6

log6.36×10-3-logK600=2.6

⇒logK600=-2.19-2.6=-4.79

⇒K600=10-4.79=1.62×10-5

=16.2×10-6

=x×10-6

⇒x=16

subject: Chemistry
q: Consider a complex reaction taking place in three steps with rate constants $k_1, k_2$ and $k_3$ respectively. The overall rate constant $k$ is given by the expression $k=\sqrt{\frac{k_1 k_3}{k_2}}$. If the activation energies of the three steps are 60,30 and $10 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}{ }^{-1}$ respectively, then the overall energy of activation in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ is $\ldots\ldots$ . (Nearest integer)
question: Consider a complex reaction taking place in three steps with rate constants $k_1, k_2$ and $k_3$ respectively. The overall rate constant $k$ is given by the expression $k=\sqrt{\frac{k_1 k_3}{k_2}}$. If the activation energies of the three steps are 60,30 and $10 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}{ }^{-1}$ respectively, then the overall energy of activation in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ is $\ldots\ldots$ . (Nearest integer)
solution:
$\mathrm{k}_1, \mathrm{k}_2$ and $\mathrm{k}_3$ are given as rate constants of three steps of a complex reaction. Rate constant (k) of the overall reaction is given as
$k=\sqrt{\frac{k_1 k_3}{k_2}}$
Activation energies of the three steps are given as
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}_1}=60 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}, \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}_2}=30 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}, \\
& \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}_3}=10 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$
From Arrhenius equation, we know that
$\mathrm{k}=\mathrm{Ae}^{-\mathrm{Ea} / \mathrm{RT} T}$
If $E_a$ is the activation energy of the overall reaction, then
$\begin{aligned}
& E_a=\frac{1}{2}\left[E_{a_1}+E_{a_3}-E_{a_2}\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{2}[60+10-30]=20 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: In a reaction $A+B \rightarrow C$, initial concentrations of $A$ and $B$ are related as $[A]_0=8[B]_0$. The half lives of $A$ and $B$ are 10 min and 40 min. respectively. If they start to disappear at the same time, both following first order kinetics, after how much time will the concentration of both the reactants be same?
question: In a reaction $A+B \rightarrow C$, initial concentrations of $A$ and $B$ are related as $[A]_0=8[B]_0$. The half lives of $A$ and $B$ are 10 min and 40 min. respectively. If they start to disappear at the same time, both following first order kinetics, after how much time will the concentration of both the reactants be same?
solution:
Given : $[\mathrm{A}]_0=8[\mathrm{~B}]_0$
$\left[\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}\right]_{\mathrm{A}}=10 \mathrm{~min}$.
$\left[\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}\right]_{\mathrm{B}}=40 \mathrm{~min}$.
$\mathrm{I}^{\text {st }}$ order kinetics
$\mathrm{t}=$ ?
$[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{t}}=[\mathrm{B}]_{\mathrm{t}}$
$-\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{A}} \times \mathrm{t} \quad-\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{B}} \times \mathrm{t}$
$\Rightarrow \quad[\mathrm{A}]_0 \mathrm{e}=[\mathrm{B}]_0 \mathrm{e}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{[\mathrm{A}]_0}{[\mathrm{~B}]_0}=\mathrm{e}^{\left(\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{A}}-\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{B}}\right) \mathrm{t}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 8=\mathrm{e}^{\left(\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{A}}-\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{B}}\right) \times \mathrm{t}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \ell \mathrm{n} 8=\left(\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{A}}-\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{B}}\right) \times \mathrm{t}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \ln 8=\ln 2\left(\frac{1}{\left(\operatorname{ta}_2\right)_{\mathrm{A}}}-\frac{1}{\left(\operatorname{ta}_2\right)_{\mathrm{B}}}\right) \times \mathrm{t}$

The order of the reaction is _____ (Nearest integer)
solution:
t12500 torr=240sec=4 min

t12250 torr =4 min.

t12∝a1-n

t121t122=a1a21-n1=20=2505001-nn=1

As t12 is independent of initial pressure. Hence, order is 1st order.

question: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, the half life for the decomposition of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AB</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and is independent of the initial concentration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AB</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The time required for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AB</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> to decompose is (Given: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>30</mn></math>; <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mn>3</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>48</mn></math>)
solution:
Since half life is independent of intial concentration of the reactant, it's a first order reaction

k=ln 2t12
kt=2.303 logA0A

ln 2t12t=2.303 logA0A

2.303 log 2200t=2.303 log10020

t=2000.3×log 5=466.67 sec≈467 sec

subject: Chemistry
q:
For bacterial growth in a cell culture, growth law is very similar to the law of radioactive decay. Which of the following graphs is most suitable to represent bacterial colony growth ?
Where N - Number of Bacteria at any time, $\mathrm{N}_0$ - Initial number of Bacteria.
question:
For bacterial growth in a cell culture, growth law is very similar to the law of radioactive decay. Which of the following graphs is most suitable to represent bacterial colony growth ?
Where N - Number of Bacteria at any time, $\mathrm{N}_0$ - Initial number of Bacteria.
solution: Because no. of bacteria initial $=\mathrm{N}_0$ and No. of bacteria at any time $t=N$ Since bacterial growth is given as $\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{Kt}}$ Where K = growth constant for bacterial growth

question:
For the reaction of H2 with I2, the rate constant is 2.5×10-4 dm3mol-1s-1 at 327oC and 1.0 dm3 mol-1s-1 at 527°C . The activation energy for the reaction, in kJ mol-1 is:

R=8.314 JK-1mol-1
solution:
T2=527oC⇒800k ;k1=2.5×10-4

T1=327oC⇒600k ;k2=1

According to Arrhenius Equation;

ln⁡k2k1=-EaR1T2-1T1

ln⁡1042.5=EaR1600-1800

ln⁡4000=Ea8.31412400

Ea=165.431 kJ, Ea≈166 kJ/mol

question:
The rate of a reaction quadruples when the temperature changes from 300  to 310 K . The activation energy of this reaction is:

(Assume Activation energy and pre-exponential factor are independent of temperature; ln(2)=0.693; R=8.314 J mol-1K-1)
solution:
The activation energy, rate constant and temperature can be related using Arrhenius equation.

k=Ae-EaRT

lnk2k1=EaR 1T1-1T2 

ln4=EaR 1300-1310 

2×0.693=EaR 10300×310 

E a  =  2×0.693×300×310×8.314 10  = 107.2 kJ/mol

subject: Chemistry
q:
At $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in presence of a catalyst, activation energy of a reaction is lowered by $10 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The logarithm of ratio of $\frac{\mathrm{k} \text { (catalysed) }}{\mathrm{k} \text { (uncatalysed) }}$ is....
(Consider that the frequency factor for both the reactions is same)
question:
At $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in presence of a catalyst, activation energy of a reaction is lowered by $10 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The logarithm of ratio of $\frac{\mathrm{k} \text { (catalysed) }}{\mathrm{k} \text { (uncatalysed) }}$ is....
(Consider that the frequency factor for both the reactions is same)
solution:
Using the Arrhenius equation: $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$
The ratio of rate constants is: $\frac{k_{catalysed}}{k_{uncatalysed}} = \frac{A e^{-E_a^c/RT}}{A e^{-E_a^u/RT}} = e^{(E_a^u - E_a^c)/RT} = e^{\Delta E_a/RT}$
Since the catalyst lowers activation energy by 10 kJ/mol: $\Delta E_a = 10$ kJ/mol = 10000 J/mol
At T = 27°C = 300 K, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K):
$\frac{k_c}{k_u} = e^{10000/(8.314 \times 300)} = e^{10000/2494.2} = e^{4.009}$
Taking logarithm base 10: $\log_{10}\frac{k_c}{k_u} = 4.009 \times \log_{10}(e) = 4.009 \times 0.4343 = 1.741$

q: If the activation energy of a reaction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the fraction of molecules at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>700</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, having enough energy to react to form products is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is (Rounded off to the nearest integer) [Use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>31</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: If the activation energy of a reaction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the fraction of molecules at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>700</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, having enough energy to react to form products is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is (Rounded off to the nearest integer) [Use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>31</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Fraction of molecules to have enough energy to react =e-Ea/RT

So, x=EaRT

=80.9×1038.31×700

=13.9≈14

subject: Chemistry
q: A molecule undergoes two independent first order reactions whose respective half lives are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math>. If both the reactions are occurring then the time taken for the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>%</mo></math> consumption of the reactant is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>min</mi></math>. (Nearest integer)
question: A molecule undergoes two independent first order reactions whose respective half lives are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math>. If both the reactions are occurring then the time taken for the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>%</mo></math> consumption of the reactant is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>min</mi></math>. (Nearest integer)
solution:
For parallel reaction

When adding the rate constants of the two reactions, we need to use the formula for combining rate constants of two reactions in parallel, which is:

k=k1+k2

where k1 and k2 are the rate constants of the individual reactions.

In this case, the half-lives of the two reactions are 12 min and 3 min, respectively. The rate constants of the two reactions can be calculated using the formula for half-life of a first-order reaction:

1t1/2=112+13=512

Net t1/2=125=2.4 min≈2 min

Hence time taken for 50% consumption of reactant will be close to 2 min.

(Rounded-off to the nearest integer) [Given : The rat5e constant of the reaction is 10-5 s-1 at 500 K.]
solution:
log k=log A-Ea2.303RT

Slope= Ea 2.303R=10,000

logk2 k1=Ea2.303R1 T1-1 T2

log10-410-5=10,0001500-1 T2

log10-410-5=10,0001500-1 T2

T2=526.31≃526 K

Hence answer is 526

subject: Chemistry
q: Consider $\mathrm{A} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{k}_{1}} \mathrm{~B}$ and $\mathrm{C} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{k}_{2}} \mathrm{D}$ are two reactions. If the rate constant $\left(\mathrm{k}_{1}\right)$ of the $\mathrm{A} \longrightarrow \mathrm{B}$ reaction can be expressed by the following equation $\log _{10} \mathrm{k}=14.34-\frac{1.5 \times 10^{4}}{\mathrm{~T} / \mathrm{K}}$ and activation energy of $C \longrightarrow D$ reaction $\left(E a_{2}\right)$ is $\frac{1}{5}$ th of the $A \longrightarrow B$ reaction $\left(E a_{1}\right)$, then the value of $\left(E a_{2}\right)$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. (Nearest Integer)
question: Consider $\mathrm{A} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{k}_{1}} \mathrm{~B}$ and $\mathrm{C} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{k}_{2}} \mathrm{D}$ are two reactions. If the rate constant $\left(\mathrm{k}_{1}\right)$ of the $\mathrm{A} \longrightarrow \mathrm{B}$ reaction can be expressed by the following equation $\log _{10} \mathrm{k}=14.34-\frac{1.5 \times 10^{4}}{\mathrm{~T} / \mathrm{K}}$ and activation energy of $C \longrightarrow D$ reaction $\left(E a_{2}\right)$ is $\frac{1}{5}$ th of the $A \longrightarrow B$ reaction $\left(E a_{1}\right)$, then the value of $\left(E a_{2}\right)$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. (Nearest Integer)
solution:
From the Arrhenius equation:
$\log_{10} k = \log_{10} A - \frac{E_a}{2.303RT}$.
Comparing with given equation $\log_{10} k_1 = 14.34 - \frac{1.5 \times 10^4}{T}$, we get: $\frac{E_{a_1}}{2.303R} = 1.5 \times 10^4$ K

$E_{a_1} = 1.5 \times 10^4 \times 2.303 \times 8.314$
$= 1.5 \times 10^4 \times 0.01913$
$= 287$ kJ/mol

Since $E_{a_2} = \frac{1}{5} E_{a_1}$:

$E_{a_2} = \frac{287}{5} = 57.4 \approx 57$ kJ/mol

subject: Chemistry
q: It is true that:
question: It is true that:
options: A second order reaction is always a multistep reaction
A zero order reaction is a multi-step reaction
A first order reaction is always a single step reaction
A zero order reaction is a single step reaction
solution: For elementary reaction, order is equal to stoichiometric coefficient. Since reaction having zero stoichiometric coefficient is meaningless therefore, zero order reaction is complex reaction or multi-step reaction.
